input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a commercial/industrial building represented as a cyber‑physical asset. It comprises architectural and infrastructure components, HVAC, lighting, power, security and control systems, as well as embedded sensors, actuators and programmable controllers. Operating in the AECO domain, the building spans design, construction, operation and refurbishment stages. The Digital Twin integrates static BIM structural and finish data with runtime sensor and log data to provide continuous real‑time monitoring, predictive analytics and decision support for building management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers real‑time monitoring of building states and asset performance; predictive analytics for maintenance and energy optimization; control services enabling facility managers to issue commands to HVAC, lighting and other building systems through the twin; immersive visualization services delivering interactive dashboards and 3D models for situational awareness; and extensible analytics by DT engineers, who can add specialized services via the framework’s extension points.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework that augments the BIM environment with device‑modeling extensions, allowing Building Control Engineers to embed sensors, actuators and controllers directly into the BIM. The framework employs Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Real‑time data exchange is supported through standardized protocols integrated into the framework, and an interactive visualization engine renders the twin in immersive interfaces. Custom service plug‑ins can be developed and integrated via defined extension points, enabling DT engineers to augment the core functionality with advanced analytics or domain‑specific services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Continuous real‑time bi‑directional data exchange is employed to keep the digital twin synchronized with its physical counterpart, enabling real‑time monitoring, control, and predictive analytics. The framework supports runtime re‑configuration of synchronization frequency to adapt to changing operational requirements, though specific latency thresholds or update intervals are not defined in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture centered around a DT Framework that serves as the central hub for bi‑directional data exchange. The framework exposes standardized extension points for integrating custom analytics and services, enabling dynamic scaling from single building components to whole‑infrastructure deployments. Roles such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers coordinate through this framework to manage device modeling, deployment, monitoring, and continuous configuration, thereby enhancing adaptability without requiring full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is accomplished via a set of standardized extension points within the DT Framework, which supports bidirectional data exchange and interoperability across heterogeneous built‑environment technologies. The framework enables modular integration of custom analytics and services, allowing seamless connection to external BIM tools, facility management software, and other Digital Twins or enterprise systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Enriched BIM models serve as the primary geometric backbone, wherein Building Control Engineers embed sensors, actuators, and controller logic directly within the Revit/BIM environment. A Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline extracts these annotations, converting them into structured representations – geometric (3‑D BIM), behavioral (control logic expressed in code or rules), and analytical (property tables) – and validates them against a reference schema before deployment. Runtime data flows are channeled through Eclipse Hono for device telemetry and Eclipse Ditto for state persistence, policy‑based validation, and event storage. Real‑time bidirectional communication guarantees that physical measurements and actuator commands are broadcast to the Digital Twin instantaneously, while persistent event logs and Ditto’s schema enforcement provide traceability and data quality. The workflow culminates in the UE 5 engine where a TwinComponent renders the geometry, a FeatureParser interprets Revit photometric metadata (e.g. lux, CRI) into light properties, and the UnrealTwinController brokers command/value updates via Ditto connections.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators are embedded in the building’s infrastructure according to the enriched BIM blueprint. Core actuators include LED lighting units that support three discrete control dimensions: on/off, dimming (0–100%), and color‑temperature adjustment (≈ 2700 K–6500 K). Additional actuators such as HVAC dampers and valves are provisioned in the BIM model (though currently only illustrative in the demo). Actuation commands are transmitted through the middleware stack: top‑level control logic publishes a command payload to DittoDevice, which propagates via DittoConnection to the linked physical device through Hono’s at‑least‑once MQTT broker. Before propagation, a policy‑based pre‑execution check validates the command against defined safe ranges, unit consistency, and conflict avoidance rules. The UnrealTwinController receives the command via UE 5 Delegates, updates the visual representation, and ensures that the physical device receives the validated instruction. Remote interfaces thus comprise MQTT topics, Ditto REST/WS APIs, and UE 5 event handlers, with systematic safety constraints enforced at every layer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensor infrastructure is defined in the BIM model; typical examples include photometric sensors on lighting fixtures and occupancy probes in occupancy beds. At runtime, sensor telemetry is streamed to Ditto over Hono’s MQTT broker and stored persistently. Ditto enforces a policy that checks that incoming values fall within expected ranges and units (e.g. lux values between 0 and 1000 lux, temperature in °C). The precision and accuracy of individual sensors are not detailed in the documentation, nor are sampling frequencies specified. Data transmission rates, spatial distribution and collection mechanisms are governed by the device’s firmware and the Hono MQTT message flow, but explicit quantitative parameters are absent from the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is achieved by enriching BIM with exact device metadata (e.g. photometric values) and by applying Model‑Driven Engineering transformations that preserve semantic relationships between geometry, control logic, and physical capabilities. Verification is performed through Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation layer, which enforces value ranges, units, and schema conformance prior to accepting sensor telemetry or actuator commands. Fault tolerance is provided by Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery semantics and Ditto’s persistent event store, ensuring that network partitions or failures do not result in data loss. Safety constraints are embedded as pre‑execution checks on actuator command payloads, preventing conflicts or unsafe operations. While explicit uncertainty quantification or sampling‑frequency coverage is not documented, the architecture’s modular extension points allow for integration of analytics that could inject statistical quality‑assurance metrics into the twin’s state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, with enriched BIM models fed into operational DTs via the modular framework’s standardised extension points, facilitating bidirectional data flows for monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
Engineering of the twin follows an MDE‑centric workflow that couples model definition, version control, and automated artifact synthesis.
The Building Control Engineer creates high‑level device models directly in BIM; these models are committed to a version control system (e.g., Git) ensuring lineage and rollback.
The framework’s MDE engine performs semantic validation, type‑checking, and consistency checks before generating deployable artifacts (e.g., containerised services, REST APIs).
Quality assurance is supported by the automated transformation pipeline that flags modeling inconsistencies and data schema violations; manual testing is facilitated by the interactive visualization UI, which exposes live sensor data streams and control interactions.
Evolution management is enabled through hot‑plugable extension points: custom plugin code can be versioned, validated, and integrated without redeploying the core DT.
Versioning of services and data schemas is tracked alongside BIM model revisions, ensuring that the twin’s state faithfully reflects the latest physical configuration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The twin's decision‑support capability is anchored in its ability to fuse real‑time sensor streams with BIM‑derived context and execute analytics pipelines.
Custom analytics services are injected via the DT Engine’s modular plugin system, allowing models such as predictive maintenance classifiers or cost‑impact estimators to consume synchronized data.
The interactive visualization component exposes control‑plane dashboards that support HMI‑driven decision making; users can drill into simulation scenarios, run “what‑if” analyses, and directly actuate control commands.
Confidence scores for recommendations are derived from the underlying model performance metrics (e.g., classifier accuracy, sensor fault detection thresholds) and are displayed alongside actionable insights, enabling Facility Managers to quantify risk before executing changes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The documents explicitly note the absence of a defined compliance framework; there is no mention of specific industry standards (e.g., IFC, Industry Foundation Classes for BIM, or IIoT interoperability standards).
The framework’s reliance on generic MDE transformations and real‑time data exchange protocols suggests potential alignment with open standards, but the implementation details and certification requirements are not described.

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs

users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic

BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational

_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their

IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
